title: Beyond average: heterogeneous first-passage dynamics in many-particle systems with resetting
authors: Juhee Lee, Seong-Gyu Yang, Ludvig Lizana
abstract: We study how stochastic resetting affects first-passage processes in systems of many interacting particles. While resetting is well understood for single-particle dynamics, its consequences for collective behavior remain less clear. We consider a protocol in which all surviving particles are reset to the position of the most extreme one, motivated by problems in artificial selection and avoidance. Using stochastic simulations of particles diffusing in a confining potential with an absorbing boundary, we examine two notions of arrival: when the first particle reaches the boundary and the point at which half of the particles do. We find that resetting produces broad distributions of arrival times with heavy tails and extended plateaus that span several orders of magnitude. As the resetting rate increases, the mean arrival time grows and diverges beyond a threshold. Trajectory-level analysis also reveals strong heterogeneity, with very short and very long absorption times. These results show that collective resetting lacks a single characteristic time scale and that the definition of arrival is crucial for understanding and controlling such systems.
summary: This paper examines how a 'group resetting' mechanism, where all particles in a system reset to the position of the most extreme particle, affects first-passage times. The researchers show that this protocol creates highly heterogeneous dynamics with arrival time distributions that span several orders of magnitude. They demonstrate that as the resetting rate increases, the mean arrival time can actually diverge. Ultimately, the study highlights that in such many-particle systems, average arrival times are insufficient to characterize the system due to the lack of a single characteristic time scale.
main_problem: Investigating how collective stochastic resetting affects first-passage dynamics and heterogeneity in many-particle interacting systems.
main_result: The study finds that group resetting produces broad hitting-time distributions with heavy tails and extended plateaus, leading to a lack of a single characteristic time scale and high trajectory heterogeneity.
method: The authors use Langevin equation simulations (Euler-Maruyama method) combined with an analytical theory based on asymptotic behaviors and extreme value theory.
model_or_system: A system of N overdamped Brownian particles in a one-dimensional harmonic potential with an absorbing boundary at the origin, subject to a group resetting protocol where all surviving particles relocate to the position of the rightmost particle.
key_observables: First Group Hitting Time (fGHT), Median Group Hitting Time (mGHT), and a Uniformity Index (w_ij) to quantify trajectory dissimilarity.
important_parameters: Resetting rate (r), group size (N), and the diffusion coefficient (D).
main_assumptions: The analytical derivation assumes the underlying single-particle dynamics follow an Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process (harmonic potential).
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a schematic of the resetting dynamics and boundary conditions; Figure 2 illustrates hitting-time distributions and the development of plateaus; Figure 3 shows position distributions of the group center of mass.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of collective resetting, defines the physical model and resetting protocol, presents numerical simulations and analytical approximations for hitting-time distributions, analyzes trajectory heterogeneity using a uniformity index, and concludes with the implications for defining characteristic time scales.
why_interesting: This is relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and many-body dynamics as it explores how non-equilibrium resetting protocols can fundamentally alter the collective transport and arrival statistics of a many-body ensemble.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic of the resetting dynamics with absorbing boundary (left vertical line) for N = 6. The particles diffuse from x0 and relocate to the rightmost particle’s position at τ. The first particle hits the boundary at τf, and half of them at τm.

FIG. 2. First (τf) and median (τm) group-hitting time distributions for N = 100 [(a) and (d)]. Symbols denote simulations and dashed lines indicate theoretical approximations, Eq. (4). Each color corresponds to a different resetting rate r. We note that the tail of the distributions extends with increasing r. Both (ensemble) averages ⟨τf⟩and ⟨τf⟩diverge for large r [(b) and (e)], but behave in the opposite way with respect to N: ⟨τf⟩decreases while ⟨τm⟩increases [(c) and (f)].
FIG. 3. Position distributions of the group mean positions ¯x(t) = P

FIG. 4. The distribution of the uniformity index wf,m ij for fGHT (a) abd mGHT (b). The distributions’ flanks rise with r, indicating that trajectories become more dissimilar (N = 100). (c,d) The distribution of τ i f,m between 0.45 < wf,m ij < 0.55 (orange shaded area) from panels (a) and (b), respectively (r = 10). The blue shaded areas are from Fig. 2(a) and (d).
